She added: “Failed asylum seekers will now be offered an increased incentive payment of £10,000 per person, and up to a maximum of £40,000 per family.

“To put that in important context, today, a family of three asylum hotel accommodation costs up to £158,000 per year.”

The Home Secretary said that if the voluntary offer is refused, there will be enforced removal for those who can return to a safe home country.

The hardline stance on immigration comes despite calls from Labour MPs for the party to move left, but do you think a £40,000 handout is a good idea? Have your say in our exclusive poll.



Love to debate? Join Sun Club for just £1.99 a month for the boldest opinion on the issues shaping Britain.

News.title: Schoolboys GUILTY of raping girl, 14, at knifepoint and filming 90 minute sex attack on second teen while laughing
News.content:
TEEN HORROR Schoolboys GUILTY of raping girl, 14, at knifepoint and filming 90 minute sex attack on second teen while laughing

TWO laughing schoolboys have been found guilty of raping a “petrified” girl in a 90-minute horror ordeal.

The teens, now aged 15, forced the girl into a “threesome” in an underpass in Fordingbridge, Hampshire, in November 2024.





They were also accused of raping a second girl in January last year along with a third boy, who was aged 13 at the time.

One of the 15-year-old boys was found guilty of one charge of rape while the other was convicted of three charges of rape in connection with the first attack.

The first defendant was also found guilty of one count of rape and the second defendant convicted of three charges of rape in connection with a second incident.

The youngest defendant, now aged 14, was found guilty of two charges of rape by encouraging the second defendant in the January incident.

The first defendant was also found guilty of taking indecent photos of a child and the second defendant previously pleaded guilty to four charges of taking indecent photos of a child.

Southampton Crown Court heard the first attack took place in November 2024 in the quiet New Forest town.

One of the boys – then aged 14 – had met up with a 15-year-old girl in a park after they began chatting on Snapchat.

Jurors were told she agreed to go to an underpass and perform sex acts on him until they were disturbed by passers-by.



The victim said she was “nervous” but “comfortable” when alone with the boy because she was feeling “some love towards” him.

But the court heard she became “scared and anxious” when the second boy, who was 14 at the time, and a third boy – not the third defendant – arrived.

Jodie Mittel KC, prosecuting, said the trio began “pressuring her and they recorded her” and they were “laughing”.

She told jurors the boys suggested they had a “threesome” which the girl “felt disgusted” about but went along with because she felt it was “the three boys against just her”.

The prosecutor added: “The word she used to describe how she was feeling was petrified.



“(She) says she did agree but only because she didn’t know what would happen if she didn’t say yes.”

The girl later described how she felt “numb” and was shaking while the boys were “laughing and recording what happened” over the 90-minute ordeal.

Ms Mittel continued: “She was feeling sick, felt like she was about to pass out, the other boys were recording whilst she sat in silence trying her best not to cry.”

Videos of the incident were later sent around and other people made jokes about the girl and she received messages calling her a “s**g”.

Ms Mittel added: “Sadly, you might think, (the two boys) didn’t seem to be very worried about what (the complainant) wanted or didn’t want to do or what she might be feeling and, we say, they just wanted to use her for the sexual things they wanted to do.”



The court was told the second girl was aged 14 when she was raped in January last year.

She told police she had been with two friends before they went home -leaving her with three other girls before the three defendants arrived.

The teen claimed the first boy threatened her with a knife and told her to leave her phone with an Airtag tracking device in a convenience store.

She was then made to walk to a recreation ground and a nearby field.

The prosecutor said video footage recorded showed the girl being raped by the first two defendants.



Two of the defendants, now aged 15, denied four counts of rape against the complainant in the first attack in November 2024.

The first defendant also faced two counts of rape and the second defendant four counts of rape against the second girl in January 2025.

A third boy, who is now 14 but 13 at the time, denied two counts of rape in the second incident by aiding and abetting the second defendant.

The first boy was also charged with taking three indecent photos of the girl in the January incident.

Judge Nicholas Rowland adjourned the case until a date to be set for sentencing.



Lucy Paddick, of the Crown Prosecution Service, said: “This is a deeply concerning case which involved a disturbing level of encouragement between young boys, who acted together to rape two young girls in separate incidents.

“These girls were forced into sexual activity by boys who brazenly filmed the deeply distressing incidents.

“After speaking with the victims in this case and reviewing an extensive file of evidence, it was the Crown Prosecution Service’s case that these girls were violated and could not have consented to these terrifying encounters.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

“The CPS worked closely with Hampshire and Isle of Wight Constabulary to support the victims and other young witnesses to give their evidence, and we commend them for the courage they showed throughout this trial.

“Our lawyers will work tirelessly to pursue justice for victims, and we will continue to hold those responsible for crimes of this devastating nature to account, regardless of their age.”

News.title: 'I chartered private jet to leave Dubai - I don't know why everyone isn't doing it'
News.content:
'I chartered private jet to leave Dubai - I don't know why everyone isn't doing it'

A British investor who claimed the state was 'safter than London' has been boasted after posting videos in a private jet back to the UK days after missile and drone attacks were launched over the Middle East

View 5 Images A British businessman in Dubai boasted after getting on a private jet to the UK while thousands struggle to get back home (Image: Facebook)

An influencer, who flogs get-rich-quick courses, has been blasted after he boasted about hiring a "£150,000" private jet out of Dubai despite claiming the UAE was "safer than London".

‌



Samuel Leeds, 34, shared videos of himself in a plane headed out of the Emirate towards Heathrow in posts people deemed "embarrassingly crude". The investor, who sells controversial online training courses to help people "make a million pounds in property & pay no tax" moved to Dubai to avoid paying "99.9 per cent of my taxes". The property man rushed back to the UK after Iran launched retaliatory strikes across the Middle East as it was hit by Israeli/US missiles.

‌



View 5 Images Samuel Leeds said he didn't know why 'everyone's not doing this' (Image: Facebook)

‌



While normal British holidaymakers and citizens in the Emirate state desperately find ways to make it back home, even waiting on flights chartered by the UK government, Leeds hired a private jet for his family.

Before boarding on the private jet, the investor claimed it was "simple" to flee Dubai. He said: "If you're trapped in Dubai, it's very simple.

View 5 Images Leeds with his wife and child on the jet (Image: Facebook)

‌



"Drive three hours to Oman, and then when you're in Oman, go down to the Muscat airport, and there's literally planes waiting for you. You can pay about £100,000, jump on a plane, and it'll take you straight to Heathrow. I don't know why everyone's not doing this."

The father-of-four then downplayed the severity of the situation, in another social media post, from his private jet to the UK. He said: "It's nowhere near as unsafe as you'd think right now in Dubai. It's not a warzone going on. The UAE government are doing a very good job of not just protecting people against missiles, but also putting people up, giving them free hotels, people that are stuck. It's not as bad as you think."

View 5 Images Fire at the US Embassy in Dubai after it was struck by a drone (Image: X)

‌



Leeds, a former magician turned "property guru", said he was flying into the UK to host a "business networking event with 1,000+ people" then would be heading back to the UAE.

Leeds added: "To be honest, it's not even dangerous in Dubai. There's more people being stabbed in England, and being shot in America, than getting hit with drones in Dubai. Right, so the only reason we're going is because I've got business to take care of, and my flight was cancelled. So stay in Dubai, but if you do need to leave, just do this."

One furious person slammed the Brit and questioned why he was flying back to the UK at this time, on Facebook. They wrote: "'If Dubai was so safe, how come you’re taking your wife and your children out of the country while bombs are going off? Would you not rather keep them in safe Dubai while you travel to dangerous England?"

Article continues below

View 5 Images Leeds flogs controversial get-rich-quick courses online (Image: Facebook)

Another person wrote: "I don't care how wealthy you are that is a ridiculous amount to pay for a flight, and to shout about it is embarrassingly crude."

This comes after British Airways issued a warning to Brits stuck in Dubai and revealed flights returning to the UK are "fully booked". The airline cautioned that all services departing from Oman, through to March 7, are now completely full. A spokesperson this afternoon said: "Flights from Muscat on 5, 6 and 7 March are now fully booked. We will continue to review the situation and, if we are able to, we will add additional services."

News.title: Police issue Ian Huntley health update one week on from brutal prison attack
News.content:
Police have issued an update after child killer Ian Huntley was beaten by a fellow inmate with a metal pole in a brutal prison workshop attack.

The 52-year-old was rushed to the Royal Victoria Infirmary (RVI) in Newcastle last Thursday following the assault at HMP Frankland. Sources told how medics "worked miracles" to save Huntley after prison staff assumed he was dead when they found him lying on the ground in a pool of blood.

The inmate who allegedly carried out the assault, triple killer Anthony Russell, is said to have claimed "I've killed him" as he was led away.

Huntley was given just a 5% chance of survival after he was reportedly struck multiple times and left with severe head injuries. Emergency services raced to the Category A prison at around 9am on February 26 before he was placed in an induced coma and rushed to hospital.

Ensure our latest headlines always appear at the top of your Google Search by making us a Preferred Source. Click here to activate or add us as your Preferred Source in your Google search settings.

In an update on his condition, police said Huntley continues to fight for his life in hospital. A spokesman for Durham Constabulary said on Thursday: "There has been no change in the 52-year-old man's condition overnight – he remains in hospital in a serious condition."

Last week, a source told how it was "extraordinary" that the killer was still alive as he was left "totally unresponsive and could not breathe" in the immediate aftermath of the attack.

"It is miraculous he is still alive," the insider told The Sun. "Medics have worked miracles on him and he has clung on. The prison nurses and staff who first saw him thought he was gone.

"And medics said there was only a five per cent chance of survival after an attack like that. It is still touch and go, and he could get worse. But it is extraordinary that he is still alive. When it first happened, he was totally unresponsive and could not breathe."

Russell is believed to have bludgeoned the child killer with a three-foot spiked metal pole, striking him at least six times and leaving him with serious head injuries.

The twisted triple killer was charged with the murder of Julie Williams and her son David Williams, as well as the brutal rape and murder of Nicole McGregor, who was pregnant at the time, near Leamington Spa in 2020. He was jailed in 2022 under a whole-life order.

Huntley is serving a life sentence after he was convicted of murdering schoolgirls Holly Wells and Jessica Chapman in August 2002. He will not be eligible for parole until at least 2042.

Police said a male prisoner in his mid-40s had been identified as a suspect by officers investigating the attack. A spokesman for Durham Constabulary said last week: "The 52-year-old prisoner who was injured during this morning’s assault in the workshop at HMP Frankland remains in a serious condition in hospital following treatment for head injuries.

"Police forensic teams have examined the scene of the attack throughout the day to gather evidence. A suspect, a male prisoner in his mid-40s, has been identified by officers investigating the incident. He has not been arrested at this stage but remains in detention within the prison."

News.title: Tourist stole Las Vegas hotel flamingo before laughing as he 'tortured' bird
News.content:
A man has been charged with animal torture after he was accused of harming a flamingo following a break-in at its enclosure at a Las Vegas hotel.

Canadian Mitchell Fairbarn was arrested at the Flamingo Hotel and Casino in Las Vegas early Tuesday morning on suspicion of multiple counts of animal cruelty. Officials disclosed they received the call regarding the alleged animal abuse at approximately 6am after a man reportedly broke into a flamingo's habitat at the hotel.

Fairbarn, 33, of Ontario, is facing four counts of felony animal abuse for the incident. CCTV caught Fairbarn entering the hotel's flamingo habitat at around 5am and grabbing a Chilean flamingo named 'Peachy'.

View 4 Images Videos and photos on Fairbarn's phone showed him carrying the animal through the hotel (Image: LVMPD)

‌



Monster who gouged out cat's eyes and left pet on doorstep being hunted by cops READ MORE :

As he tried to remove the bird from the enclosure, the Canadian is said to have caused injury to a number of birds, including one he 'pinned down' and didn't allow to escape. CCTV footage then shows him getting into a lift holding a bird, wrapped in a shirt, before wrangling it by the neck and legs in the corridor as he walks back to his room.

The police report states: "Fairbarn is seen laughing in the video and says 'I'm taking it home' as he walks back to his room, depicting a big 'Fairbarn' tattoo on his upper back, and the video ends." Fairbairn told police he entered the flamingo enclosure after spotting a flamingo in distress, reports the Daily Star.

View 4 Images Fairbarn is seen laughing in videos saying he was going to 'take the animal home' (Image: LVMPD)

He informed authorities he went into the habitat and attempted to pop the flamingo's wing back into place despite multiple warning signs prohibiting public access to the enclosure. Police said: "He state he knows that 'popping' the wing back into its place [is] a common practice for birds, such as ducks. He has knowledge of this because he is a farm boy."

‌



According to animal control, Peachy the flamingo is said to have suffered a wing injury after the wing was "pulled" from its body, resulting in the animal needing stitches. At least two other flamingos in the habitat were injured.

Inside his hotel room, police later found a "large bloody feather", along with other material. Authorities have stated Fairbairn "willfully and maliciously severely injured a federally protected migratory species such as the flamingo."

View 4 Images Fairbarn is now facing four felony charges of animal abuse (Image: LVMPD)

‌



The arrest report stated: "In addition to Fairbarn having on his cellphone multiple videos and pictures of him torturing a flamingo in his hotel room, by choking it and throwing it to the ground multiple times, while he is laughing about it, and with absolutely no regard for the safety and wellness of the animals, he was arrested and charged with four counts of Willful/ Malicious Torture/ Maim/ Kill an Animal."

Fairbairn is due to appear in court on Monday, March 9. He has been ordered to stay away from the Las Vegas Strip after posting a $12,000 (£9,000) bail on Thursday.

On Wednesday, Caesars Entertainment Corp., which owns the Flamingo hotel, issued a statement on the incident it called "deeply distressing". It read: "Our beloved birds, including Peachy, who was cruelly taken from the habitat, are currently in the care of dedicated veterinarians and our extraordinary Flamingo animal care team. We are hopeful they will make a full recovery."

News.title: Boy, 9, died 'in agony' after operation at NHS hospital as surgeon suspended
News.content:
Boy, 9, died 'in agony' after operation at NHS hospital as surgeon suspended

Nine-year-old Jack Moate died 'in agony' after a hip operation at NHS Addenbrooke’s Hospital in Cambridge by a now-suspended orthopaedic surgeon, his mum has said

View 5 Images Jack Moate, nine, died after an operation at NHS Addenbrooke’s Hospital (Image: PA)

A mum has told how her nine-year-old son died 'in agony' after an operation at an NHS hospital.

‌



Jack Moate was operated on by Kuldeep Stohr, an orthopaedic consultant who has since been suspended, at Addenbrooke’s Hospital in Cambridge on September 28, 2015. He died less than two months later after the eight-hour surgery left him with "significant blood loss", his right hip wrongly aligned, and his left hip unstable and unhealed.

‌



Nearly 10 years on, his case has now been referred to a coroner after a review into Ms Stohr's practices found "evidence that fatal physical harm was caused".

‌



View 5 Images Elizabeth said her son died 'in agony' (Image: Family Handout/PA Wire)

Somerset hospital evacuated after blaze breaks out in ward READ MORE :

Evidence Jack suffered 'fatal harm' at hands of surgeon

Jack, who had multiple complex medical needs and limited mobility, had been referred for the operation to correct his hips. He died on November 21, 2015, with his cause of death was recorded at the time as deep vein thrombosis and cerebral palsy, alongside epilepsy.

A letter from Cambridge University Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust (CUH) to Jack’s mother Elizabeth Moate last month said that an independent clinical expert who reviewed Jack’s care found "there was evidence that fatal physical harm was caused".

‌



It read: "In the NHS, fatal harm means at the time of reporting, the patient, in this case Jack, has died and the incident may have contributed to the death.”

It continued that "we have reported Jack’s case to the coroner", who would be in contact with Ms Moate "to discuss next steps".

The expert also found that “the care provided was not in line with the standards we expect”.

‌



Jack’s case was reviewed as part of a wider external clinical review commissioned by the trust last year into the practice of Ms Stohr amid concerns about care that was “below the expected standard”.

View 5 Images Kuldeep Stohr has been named as the suspended surgeon (Image: Sky News)

Review of almost 700 patients

The trust said last March that the care of “almost 700 patients who have undergone planned surgical procedures” would be reviewed.

‌



Concerns were raised as early as 2015 and were the subject of an external clinical review in 2016.

A separate independent review, which reported back last year, highlighted how the 2016 review raised concerns about Ms Stohr’s surgical technique and judgment but it was “misunderstood” and opportunities to act on the findings were “missed”.

Jack’s mother, who lives in Cambridgeshire, said her son had epilepsy and was largely confined to a wheelchair, although he could sit independently and unaided on the floor without any back support, and was able to stand in a standing frame and use a walking machine.

‌



He had regular physiotherapy, which sometimes caused pain, but he mainly coped well with his many life-limiting conditions, and was able to understand commands and communicate using facial expressions and noises.

Ms Moate said she was concerned about him undergoing surgery due to his complex conditions and him being highly susceptible to infections.

View 5 Images Jack suffered "significant blood loss" (Image: Family Handout/PA Wire)

‌



'They sent my boy home and he died in agony'

Jack suffered "significant blood loss" in the procedure, which ultimately left his right hip wrongly aligned and his left hip unstable and unhealed.

Ms Moate said: "I’ll never be able to forgive the hospital for what happened. They sent my boy home and he died in agony.

"I said I didn’t want him to have surgeries on both hips at the same time. I’d have much rather him had one operation at a time. I felt pressured into signing the consent form."

‌



She said that she wanted !answers and justice for Jack".

Solicitor Elizabeth Maliakal, of Hudgell Solicitors, who represents Ms Moate, said Jack’s death "pre-dates all investigations and reviews carried out into Ms Stohr’s practice".

"Had it been properly and fully investigated at the time, action to address her shortcomings could have been taken then, and we may well not be where we are now, with more than 700 cases being reviewed," she said.

‌



View 5 Images Jack's mum says she didn't want him to have surgery on both hips at the same time (Image: PA)

She has called for a statutory inquiry. "There is a wider pressing hospital patient safety issue to be addressed around the management and governance of specialist surgeons," she said.

"We want a meeting with (Health Secretary) Wes Streeting as a matter of urgency."

‌



Dr Broster added: "Our thoughts remain with his family, and we are doing everything we can to support them at this extremely difficult time.

"We have passed all details of his care at the trust to the coroner to investigate the cause of Jack’s death and will continue to provide full assistance to them.

"The external retrospective clinical review, led by Andrew Kennedy KC, into the cases of patients who received care by Kuldeep Stohr remains ongoing and we continue to focus on supporting all patients and families affected.

Article continues below

"We will publish a summary of the review findings once all cases have been reviewed and discussed with those affected, and remain committed to implementing improvements to our services to ensure that cases like Jack’s cannot happen again."

Ms Stohr was removed from clinical practice in 2024 and subsequently suspended. She remains suspended.

It is expected that the clinical review process will conclude this summer, with the trust set to publish findings and any recommendations.

News.title: Stephen King shares his top 10 favourite books including Western masterpiece
News.content:
The maestro of terror is the genius behind countless classics - It, The Shining and Carrie, to name but a few. However, when Stephen King isn't crafting new masterpieces, he's an avid reader. The esteemed author has revealed that he typically devours around 80 books annually, commenting: "So many books, so little time."

But which works does he hold in the highest regard? For The Top Ten: Writers Pick Their Favourite Books, King unveiled his choices along with a poignant anecdote about his top pick.

He recounted a treasured memory from his hometown of Lisbon Falls, Maine. With only a few dollars to his name at a discount shop, he risked more than half his budget on a book he dubbed "the best $2.25 I ever spent."

News.title: Police make fourth arrest in decades old Lisa Dorrian murder case
News.content:
Police make fourth arrest in decades old Lisa Dorrian murder case

Police have confirmed a fourth arrest in connection with the case of Lisa Dorrian, who went missing from a caravan park in 2005

View 2 Images Lisa Dorrian vanished on February 27, 2005 (Image: PA)

Police investigating the murder of a woman more than 20 years ago have made a fourth arrest.

Lisa Dorrian went missing from a party at Ballyhalbert Caravan Park, in Northern Ireland. Despite extensive searches in multiple areas and a fierce campaign by her family, her body has never been found.

Police believe the 25-year-old was murdered on the night of February 27, 2005, or in the early hours of the following morning.

Ensure our latest headlines always appear at the top of your Google Search by making us a Preferred Source. Click here to activate or add us as your Preferred Source in your Google search settings.

As of Thursday (March 5), police confirmed the arrest of a fourth person in connection with the case. A 48-year-old man was arrested in Craigavon, Northern Ireland, on suspicion of murder, assisting offenders and preventing a lawful and decent burial, reports Belfast Live.

The arrest on March 5 is the fourth recent arrest police have made in relation to the investigation.

View 2 Images Her body was tragically never found (Image: PA)

A woman, aged 40, and a man, aged 42, were arrested on February 25 on suspicion of murder, assisting offenders, withholding information and preventing a lawful and decent burial were later released on bail pending further enquiries. A 40-year-old man was also arrested on Wednesday, (March 4), on suspicion of assisting offenders and withholding information. He was later released on bail pending further enquiries.

Detective Chief Inspector Kerrie Foreman said: “Twenty-one years ago, a loving family were robbed of their daughter and sister.

“Lisa, who was from Bangor, was last seen alive on the night of Sunday, 27 February 2005, in Ballyhalbert. It’s believed she was murdered that night or in the early hours of the following morning.

“Our investigation is ongoing, and we remain determined to provide answers, closure and justice.”

Anyone with information is asked to contact detectives on 101.

Article continues below

Information can also be provided to the independent charity Crimestoppers, anonymously, on 0800 555 111 or online at www.crimestoppers-uk.org.

News.title: Dunelm £14 pillows beat posh rivals in blind test - 'total game changer'
News.content:
Dunelm £14 pillows beat posh rivals in blind test - 'total game changer'

Dunelm have been announced as the best 'budget' option for pillows- and they're the perfect pair for those who tend to sleep on their back throughout the night

View 2 Images The high street pillows were rated as the best budget option (Image: Guido Mieth via Getty Images)

Finding the perfect pillow is a nightmare of trial and error, usually ending in a stiff neck and a ruined morning. But if you're a back-sleeper on a budget, the search is over.

‌



After extensive testing, Dunelm's £14 Fogarty Soft Cotton pillows have been crowned the best value buy. They aren't fancy to look at, but for a high-street price, they deliver high-end sleep without the "budget" aches.

‌



Offering a medium level of support, they're designed for those who sleep on their back for the majority of the night. So, if you are looking to replace your old pillows on a tight budget, Dunelm have shoppers covered.

‌



View 2 Images The pillows are only £14 and are perfect for those who are on a budget (Image: Amazon)

According to the Guardian, who put 10 different pillows to the test, they found that while these pillows are advertised for back sleepers, they were " comfortable to use in all sleeping positions".

But added: " However, I'd suggest the level of support and height is better suited to those who sleep predominantly on their back and only occasionally on their side. If you spend most of the night on your side, you'll likely prefer something taller and firmer, such as the Silentnight below.

‌



"The 100% recycled polyester filling in the Fogarty pillows doesn’t initially feel different from any other cheap pillows. However, during testing, it felt wonderfully soft and cosy, and I didn’t overheat or get too sweaty, which I feared may be a problem. The cover is made entirely from cotton, and the pillows can be machine washed and tumble dried on a low heat."

On the Dunelm website, happy shoppers have rated them an impressive 4.3 out of 5, with one person saying: "These pillows are absolutely lovely. Poofy but not too high rise so that they hurt the neck. My husband thinks they are perfect and that in itself is testament to the quality and comfort. Recommend."

Another added: "Probably the best nights deep sleep I have had in many years. Give them a try - you won't regret it. Can't wait to get to bed again."

Article continues below

However one person pointed out: "While they provided good back support for a couple of months, recently the inner pads gets twisted and deformed within the fabric liner. Every couple of days I have to manipulate the pad back into shape which is becoming harder to do. I might need to cut it open and resew it back."

According to the Guardian review, the best all-round rated pillow was the Otty Deluxe Pure pillow (£69). The memory foam pillow " conform to your head and neck, and it offers a superb balance of comfort and support, making it a great choice for a range of sleeping positions." Striking a "happy middle ground" when it comes to firm yet cushioned, it's reported to be supportive and bouncy.

Do you have a story to share? Email niamh.kirk@reachplc.com

News.title: 'I’m a forensic expert and there was missing evidence in Jeffrey Epstein’s death scene'
News.content:
'I’m a forensic expert and there was missing evidence in Jeffrey Epstein’s death scene'

A forensic psychologist has reviewed the DOJ Inspector General's report on Jeffrey Epstein's death and highlights 'structurally compromised' evidence including falsified prison logs and missing surveillance footage

'I’m a forensic psychologist and there was missing evidence in Epstein’s death scene'

A forensic psychologist has examined official findings surrounding Jeffrey Epstein's death, highlighting significant gaps in documentation - describing the case as "cover-up compatible" due to what she terms a "structurally compromised evidence record".

‌



Dr Tracy King, a forensic psychologist and expert witness commentator, scrutinised publicly available documents, including the United States Department of Justice Office of the Inspector General's report into the Federal Bureau of Prisons' custody and oversight of Epstein at the Metropolitan Correctional Center in New York.

‌



"The reason Epstein's death still generates debate is because the evidence architecture appears structurally compromised," she states in her analysis. "[It's] not one gap. [It's] a pattern of gaps. A stack of failures moving in the same direction."

‌



Falsified records and monitoring lapses

View 3 Images Epstein's death scene was "cover-up-compatible," said Dr King (Image: 60 Minutes/CBS)

The initial inconsistencies she identifies concern the monitoring failures. She observes, following her review of the Inspector General's conclusions, that mandatory checks were not performed as stipulated, and that certain documentation was subsequently altered to suggest proper procedures had been followed, reports the Mirror US.

Given this, she argues that "once records have been falsified, we lose the ability to use those documents as an objective timeline. The credibility of authorities is reduced."

‌



Dr King notes that when official records cannot be deemed trustworthy, investigators are forced to depend on inference and memory.

She explained: "In practical terms, it means the account has to rely more heavily on inference and witness memory, rather than hard contemporaneous documentation. In plain language, the state lost control of its own evidence record."

She added that compromised timelines may result in diminished faith in official findings, including his formally recorded cause of death. "Reliable timelines are one of the foundations of confidence. If the timeline is polluted, conclusions become weaker."

‌



Missing clip

View 3 Images Multiple records claim Jeffrey Epstein's death scene was full of inconsistencies (Image: DOJ)

Another key area of concern, according to Dr King, centres on the surveillance problems highlighted in the Inspector General's report - including documented deficiencies in camera recording capability at the facility.

"The second anchor of certainty in any custodial death should be surveillance. Video does not replace investigation, but it provides a neutral record," she explains.

‌



In particular, she highlights footage purportedly relating to the evening of Epstein's death, which features a missing portion of approximately 23 seconds.

She emphasises that "the issue is not the number of seconds. The issue is whether investigators can show the footage is complete and trustworthy."

She insists that a series of technical queries need definitive responses to instil confidence, including details about which camera captured the footage, whether the gap was present in the original system or occurred during duplication, who had access to the file, and if an independent review of the original recording took place.

‌



"Essentially I am saying: Can investigators prove the video is complete and untampered with, from the moment it was recorded to the moment it was released?".

Risk Reduction

View 3 Images Jeffrey Epstein's 2019 death was ruled a suicide (Image: DOJ)

Dr King states it remains uncertain whether appropriate operational safeguards were in place to minimise risk within the institution. She points out that the Inspector General's report reveals numerous shortcomings in terms of risk reduction and supervision.

‌



From a psychological perspective, she notes the significant interaction between an individual with elevated risk factors, such as Epstein, and weakened institutional safeguards.

The expert says: "This combination keeps two competing narratives plausible in the public mind: Narrative A: suicide occurred, enabled by institutional failure. Narrative B: something more organised occurred, enabled by institutional failure."

Without pointing fingers at specific individuals, she introduces the notion of an environment she describes as "cover-up compatible," due to its failure to provide sufficient clear evidence.

Article continues below

"When I say cover up compatible, I am describing an evidential environment, not accusing specific people, as that is the thing, the evidence is too foggy to do so.

"Concealment, if it occurs, often exploits pre-existing weaknesses rather than requiring large coordinated plots. A weak system can create ambiguity that looks the same as concealment.

"The public cannot easily tell the difference when the evidence system itself is compromised. It creates a 'we will never know for sure' scenario."
